title: Unit 33 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Irregular and Defective Words

script.text: Welcome... to the final Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 33: Irregular and Defective Words. This is the finish line. We’ve fought through Cases, Tenses, and Aspects... and today, we tackle the rebels of the Greek language. The words that looked at the rulebook and decided to throw it away. I'm William, and for one last time, I'm joined by our native expert, Athina. Athina... how does it feel to be at the end of the journey?

Honestly, I am so proud of everyone. Greek is ancient and complex, but once you reach this level, you start to see the beauty in its imperfections. These 'Irregular' words aren't just mistakes; they are fossils. They are pieces of history that survived while everything else changed.
Fossils... I love that. Let's dig into our first group: The Gender Benders. These are masculine nouns ending in
that... for some reason... become Neuter in the plural. Athina, give us the most famous one.
Masculine singular: The time or the year. But when we talk about many years, we don't say
Wait... 'Πολλά χρόνια'... that sounds like a Neuter plural adjective and a Neuter plural noun. So the 'rhyme' we learned in Unit 6 still works, but only because the noun itself changed gender?
In the singular, it’s a reason or a word. But in the plural... it has two lives! If you mean 'reasons', you stay masculine:
But if you mean 'spoken words' or 'speech'... it becomes plural Neuter:
So if I want to tell someone 'Your words are beautiful', I say:
Fascinating. Now... let's talk about the complete outsiders. Group 2: The Indeclinables. These words are like visitors who never change their clothes. Athina... who are these rebels?
These are usually loanwords from French or English. Words like
jazz. No matter if they are the Subject, Object, or Genitive... the word stays exactly the same. Only the article changes to tell you the case.
In English, that sounds normal... but in Greek, where everything usually declines, it feels like cheating!
It is a little bit! But look out for feminine names too. Many names like
used to decline more in the past... but today, many speakers keep them simple in the Accusative. For loanwords? Zero change. Guaranteed.

Pro Tip: If you're stuck and don't know the declension... maybe just say it in English and hope it’s a loanword! Just kidding. Let's move to Section 3: The Defective Verbs. These are verbs that are... well... missing parts. Athina, why are they broken?
They aren't broken; they are just 'Continuous' by nature. Take the verb
to belong. You can only 'be belonging' to something. It doesn't have a 'momentary' version. There is no Aorist. The same goes for
Aha! So you can't use the Simple Future with a new stem... because the stem doesn't exist. You always use the Present stem.
Perfect. And then there are the 'Impersonal' verbs. They only exist in the third person singular.
It must. It is needed. It seems. They have no 'I' or 'You' forms.
Finally... let’s look at the 'Relics'. The words that come straight from Ancient Greek but are still used in daily idioms. Like
It means 'Behold!'. We have a very famous saying:
Ιδού η Ρόδος, ιδού και το πήδημα.
Behold Rhodes, behold the jump! It means: 'Don't just talk about it, show me what you can do!'
I love that. 'Show me the jump'. Well, listeners... you have made the jump. You have officially completed the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass series for Modern Greek Grammar.
Congratulations! You have the foundation now to speak, read, and understand this beautiful language. Practice every day.
Excellent work. This concludes Unit 33. Go out there and use it. Ya sas!
Γεια σας και καλή συνέχεια! Τα καταφέρατε!
